title: Cat Hard Crusty Overgrowth on Paw Pads: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Identify hard, horn-like growths on your cat's paw pads. Learn why these cutaneous horns occur and when you should consult a veterinarian.
content_html:
What are these hard growths on my cat's paws?

Finding a hard, crusty protrusion on your cat's paw pad can be startling. These are most commonly known as cutaneous horns. They are composed of an overgrowth of keratin—the same protein that makes up your cat's claws and hair. These growths often resemble a small, curved "extra claw" or a hard callous protruding from the fleshy part of the pad.

Is this a medical emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is generally Low. Cutaneous horns are typically benign and painless. However, you should monitor the area for changes. If the growth is accompanied by bleeding, a foul odor, swelling, or if your cat starts limping, it is time to schedule a routine veterinary appointment. While usually harmless, these growths can sometimes be associated with underlying conditions like Feline Leukemia Virus (FeLV), so a check-up is always a good idea.

Why taking a photo helps

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's paw pad is an excellent first step for triage. A high-quality image allows your veterinarian to assess the texture, size, and color of the growth remotely. This helps them determine if the growth is a simple keratin plug or something that requires more immediate diagnostic testing, such as a biopsy or a blood test, saving you time and helping prioritize your cat's care.

faqs.question: Should I try to trim the growth at home?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to trim the growth yourself the first time you see it. A veterinarian needs to ensure there is no blood supply or nerve ending involved before any grooming is attempted.

faqs.question: Can cutaneous horns be cured?
faqs.answer: While the horn itself can be trimmed back, the underlying cause of the keratin overgrowth often remains, meaning the horn may grow back over time.

faqs.question: Are these growths contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: No, cutaneous horns themselves are not contagious. However, if the horns are caused by an underlying viral infection like FeLV, that virus can be transmitted between cats.
